does: This paper investigates emergent alignment in large language models by finetuning a helpful-only model on narrow safety tasks using Constitutional AI with four distinct ethical frameworks: deontology, consequentialism, virtue ethics, and human-authority alignment. The key finding is that training on just two narrow safety subcategories reliably induces aligned behavior across broader safety categories and even on safety subcategories explicitly excluded from the training data. The authors demonstrate this phenomenon systematically across these four ethical personas, showing that finetuning on constrained domains produces generalized safety improvements. This work provides empirical evidence for the persona selection mechanism hypothesis by demonstrating that safety properties can transfer and emergently arise beyond their training distribution, suggesting that language models learn generalizable ethical principles rather than merely memorizing narrow task associations.
connects: This work builds directly on research into emergent misalignment, which demonstrated that narrow task finetuning can paradoxically induce broad behavioral problems in language models. It engages with the persona selection mechanism hypothesis proposed to explain this phenomenon, which posits that LLMs during pretraining learn to simulate diverse characters and worldviews that can be selectively elicited during posttraining. The paper also extends the Constitutional AI framework, which has previously shown that finetuning with explicit ethical principles can improve alignment. By investigating the converse of emergent misalignment through multiple ethical frameworks simultaneously, this work connects emerging understanding of how finetuning shapes model behavior with classical ethical theory, bridging alignment research with philosophy to understand how different moral systems might be embedded in and generalized by neural networks.
enables: This research opens several future directions for alignment research. It suggests that alignment might be achievable through learning generalizable ethical principles rather than exhaustively specifying desired behavior across all scenarios, potentially scaling alignment methods to handle novel situations. The finding that multiple ethical frameworks can be independently encoded and elicit broad safety improvements raises questions about composing or selecting among different ethical personas for different contexts. It enables investigation into whether language models can learn to reason about moral philosophy itself, and whether explicit constitution-based training provides a more interpretable and verifiable alignment method than end-to-end optimization. The work motivates new desiderata for alignment focused on projectability of learned ethical personas, suggesting future research into understanding what makes certain safety properties generalize while others remain narrow, with implications for developing more robust and comprehensive alignment techniques.
